coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots for staining bacterial dead cells and distinguishing dead/alive bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yuting
Xu, Yuanhong
Wen, Qin
coredata.dc:description: The small molecular dyes such as propidium iodide (PI) always suffer from photo-bleaching and potential toxicity. To tackle the problems, a type of nontoxic carbon dots (CDs) was obtained for dead/alive bacterial distinguishing. This kind of carbon dots has an average size of 1.91 nm and owns carboxyl groups, emerging as excellent candidates for imaging bacterial cells. The negative charges of carboxyl groups lead their avoidance of alive cells while their small size facilitates penetration of dead cells. This kind of nontoxic CDs has effectively differentiated between and alive ones, presenting a highly promising green dye comparing with traditional small molecular dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bacterial imaging
Bacterial monitoring
Bacterial discrimination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bacterial imaging and alive/dead bacterial distinguishing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274084 ), Natural Science Foundation of Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province ( ZR2021JQ04 ), Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province (No. Tsqn 201909088 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scheme of distinguishing alive/dead bacterial cells using as-prepared carbon dots.

The emission spectra and maximum excitation/emission spectra (a), fluorescence lifetime (b), CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram corresponding to the emission spectra (c) and the intensity data of continuous irradiation under 365 nm (d) for carbon dots; Changes of fluorescence intensity under different concentration of sodium chloride (e) and different pH (f) for carbon dots.
(a) TEM image and size statistics, (b) HRTEM image, FT-IR spectra (c) for carbon dots.
XPS full spectrum(a), high-res XPS spectrum of C (b), N (c), and O (d) for carbon dots.

Bright field image, green channel (apply to FTIC, excitation wavelength was to around 480 nm) for fluorescence image, red channel (apply to PI, excitation wavelength was around 540 nm) for fluorescence image and merged of the three mentioned above for dead cells only and distinguish dead and alive bacterial cells.

Alamar blue cellular viability assay (a), ROS level performed by fluorescent intensity (b), and spread plate method for bacterial survival (c) after treated with white light radiation for 10 min at 250 W.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots for staining bacterial dead cells and distinguishing dead/alive bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Science, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Engineering, College of Life Science, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The small molecular dyes such as propidium iodide (PI) always suffer from photo-bleaching and potential toxicity. To tackle the problems, a type of nontoxic carbon dots (CDs) was obtained for dead/alive bacterial distinguishing. This kind of carbon dots has an average size of 1.91 nm and owns carboxyl groups, emerging as excellent candidates for imaging bacterial cells. The negative charges of carboxyl groups lead their avoidance of alive cells while their small size facilitates penetration of dead cells. This kind of nontoxic CDs has effectively differentiated between and alive ones, presenting a highly promising green dye comparing with traditional small molecular dyes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection can arise numerous diseases [1–3], especially for individuals with underlying health conditions [3,4]. Studies indicated that nosocomial infections could spread through dry surface [4] and some bacteria such as methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus can easily disseminate within medical facility [5]. Herein, early observation and diagnosis hold immense importance in the realm of prevention and treatment of bacterial as they can alleviate patients’ suffering, reduce treatment cost and conservation of medical resources.

Among various detecting methods [6–10], fluorescent imaging features for providing visible data compared to others, with the detecting time usually under 30 min [11,12]. Small molecular dyes are the most common staining agents for bacteria. However, they face challenges such as high cost, photobleaching and potential genotoxicity [13,14]. Differently, carbon dots (CDs) represent a class of zero-dimensional ultrasmall carbon-based materials with an average size below 10 nm [15–19], which owns inherent photoluminescence, excellent photo stability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and easy surface modification characteristics. CDs have been applied widely in imaging mammal cells and tissues for biomedical field due to their easy preparation, modification for specific needs, and abundant active groups, such as amino, carboxyl or hydroxyl, on outer shells [11,20–25]. However, staining bacterial cells are still challenging when compared with staining mammal cells since smaller size of bacteria. Plus the thick cell walls of bacteria, containing interlaced peptidoglycan, teichoic acid or lipopolysaccharide, make them harder be penetrated [26,27]. Relatively small volume, abundant functional groups or electrostatic interaction may be the reasons for CDs to go through the bacteria [12,26,28–30].

In this work, a one-pot hydrothermal method was utilized to synthesize a metal-free carbon dots with an average particle size of 1.91 nm. The fluorescent properties, morphology and functional groups of CDs were investigated, indicating ultrasmall size and surface functional groups may contribute to the dead cell staining capacity of the carbon dots. Fig. 1 depicted the mechanism of bacterial imaging using as-prepared carbon dots. The carboxyl group provided negative charge and the carbon dots is extremely small, so it can come inside the dead bacterial cells. As a result, the carbon dots have successfully discriminated the dead/alive bacterial cells via fluorescent imaging, showcasing their potential dye for bacteria and other types of cells.

Citric acid (CA), l-arginine (Arg), sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, acetic acid, sodium acetate, sodium bicarbonate, sodium carbonate, sodium hydroxide, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane, glycine, sodium chloride and quinine were brought from Macklin Co. Ltd. 3 % benzalkonium bromide were purchased from Shandong Lierkang medical technology Co., Ltd. Yest extract and tryptone were purchased from OXOID Ltd. Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC25923) were purchased from Beyotime Ltd. Reverse osmosis water were used to dissolve chemicals expect bacterial relative reagent such as fluid nutrient medium. For culturing and washing bacterial cells, sterile water was employed. Fluorescent properties were detected by a FS5 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK). Images of transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were captured using a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope at an acceleration voltage of 80 kV or 200 kV. Infrared spectra were gathered by a IS50 Fourier-transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using a ESCALAB Xi + spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd.) with a monochromatic Al Kα (1486.6 eV) source. Bacterial images were shot using an Eclipse Ni–U (Nikon Ltd. Japan) fluorescence microscope. Zeta potential and hydrated size were detected by a Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., U.K.). Alamar blue assay kit was purchased from Yeasen biotech Co. Ltd. reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay kit was purchased from Beyotime Ltd.

Carbon dots were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.288 g citric acid plus 1.04 g arginine were diluted into 15 mL water solution, then put it into 30 mL Teflon reactor and kept at 180 °C for 6 h. Citric acid and arginine should be totally dissolved before reacting. When the temperature of reaction mixture drop down to room temperature naturally, the mixture was filtered by 0.22 μm membrane and then dialyzed using RO water for 24 h with a 500 Da dialysis bag. Then, the dialyzed liquid was collected for vacuum freezing dying. Finally, the freeze-dried powder was kept in room temperature away from light.

Maximum excitation/emission wavelength was detected by a FS5 spectrophotometer. The influence of fluorescent intensity by pH was practiced by dissolving the identical volume of carbon dots into the identical volume of buffer with different pH value. Buffers are all 0.1 mol/L. The effect of sodium chloride (0∼2.4 mol/L for 9 concentration gradient, step = 0.3 mol/L), was studied with similar method. Continuous irradiation experiment was conducted with a 365 nm lamp for irradiating for 3 h and the intensity data were recorded every 15 min. Instant stability experiment was performed by collecting the fluorescence signal for 20 times without interval time.

The QY of the carbon dots was practiced by a relative quantum yield calculating method [31]. In this method, quinine sulfate as a reference standard with 0.54 QY value when the excitation light is around 345 nm. To be specific, 0.0157 g quinine sulfate was diluted into 20 mL 0.1 mol/L sulfuric acid. And the carbon dots solution was prepared at least for five different concentrations with a concentration gradient of c, 0.8 c, 0.6 c, 0.4 c and 0.2 c (set ‘c’ as the highest concentration of the 5 tested solution). Both the quinine and the carbon dots solution were no more than 0.1 or 0.05 under the ultraviolet–visible light under corresponding the value of excitation wavelength. The equation was followed as:
Ф x
=
Ф
S T
(
G r a d
x
G r a d
S T
)
(
η x 2
η
S T
2
)
where the x and ST refer to text solution and reference solution separately. Ф represent QY value. Grad means the slope of the integrated fluorescent intensity dividing the corresponding absorbance testing by ultraviolet–visible light. And η refers to solvent refractive index.

For bacterial culturing, the bacterial freeze-dried powder was diluted. Then, streak plate was employed to obtain the growth of the single bacterial colonies. Pick up 3 or 4 single colonies for culturing in liquid culture medium at 37 °C with 150 rpm (rotate speed per minute). After culturing overnight (usually 10 h), the cloudy liquid (OD600 = 1.2–1.3 which equal to 3 × 109 CFU/mL) was obtained before use. For the preparation of dead bacterial cells, 3 % benzalkonium bromide was employed to treat the alive cells for around 60 s [29,30]. Subsequently, the mixture was washed using phosphate buffer system (PBS) for three times for 3 min at 3500 rpm. After washing out the cultural medium, the pellet was redispersed in PBS before use. For bacterial fluorescence staining, around 500 μg/mL carbon dots colloid were prepared then cultured with dead cells for 30 min or 1 h. After these, we washed out the carbon dots colloid and redisperse the cell pellet into PBS. Finally, 3 μL of mixture was sucked up to drop onto the glass slide coating with cover glass before observed.

To evaluate the phototoxicity for bacteria, a xenon lamp with white light (type: BBZM-Ⅰ, 250W) was employed. Carbon dots were mixed with 1.5 × 107 CFU/mL bacteria under 1:1 vol firstly. After radiating for 10 min, spread plate method, cellular viability and levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) were performed. The concentrations of bacteria were 1000, 500, 250, 125 and 0 μg/mL. Cellular viability was carried out using alamar blue assay. Oxidative alamar blue has no fluorescence unless they react with coenzyme or dehydrogenase inside the cells and turned themselves into fluorescent reductive alamar blue ((λ(ex) = 530, λ(em) = 590). In brief, 100 % reductive alamar blue was prepared firstly. And the viability ratio was represented by the fluorescent value of test group dividing by reductive 100 % alamar blue. Besides, ROS level was also detected. Fluorescence probe was firstly uploaded into cells, and 10 min radiation of white light was performed. Subsequently, fluorescent intensity was recorded by a multifunctional enzyme marking instrument.

Maximum excitation/emission spectra, emission spectrum and fluorescent lifetime were determined firstly. As shown in Fig. 2 a, maximum excitation of carbon dots peaked at 360 nm which corresponds to 445 nm as maximum emission value. The excitation wavelength was picked up from 300 nm to 450 nm (step = 10 nm) to give out the corresponding emission spectra. As Fig. 2a described, maximum excitation wavelength was around 370 nm according with the maximum excitation-emission spectrum. Once the value of excitation wavelength was bigger than 370 nm, the emission scan showed red shift and the intensity of emission went down. The same trend of changes of emission peak value starting from visible light would be observed as Fig. 2c. When excited at 300 nm, CDs showed blue emission, and the luminescence gradually shift to green when the excitation wavelength climbed to 450 nm and this color change was consistent with the spectra in Fig. 2a. In addition, fluorescent lifetime was calculated to be 7.8 ns (Fig. 2b). The stability of carbon dots solution was also evaluated. The continuous irradiation experiment was performed as Fig. 2d shown (n = 3). In the initial 15 min irradiation, the fluorescent intensity decreased fast to 70 % comparing with the initial value. After 15 min, the florescent intensity had slight changes till to 3 h, indicating that the carbon dots had a relative stable intensity under constant 365 nm light irradiation. Moreover, intensity changes of constant irradiating were carried out. As Fig. S1 shown, the intensity is reasonable and stable after 20 times constant testing. Different pH and sodium chloride concentrations were also considered to assess stability for complicated applicable scene. As Fig. 2e depicted, sodium chloride would result in the fluorescent intensity decline to around 80 %, but the intensity values were at least 80 % of the control group under all concentration of sodium chloride. The pH range for application is 2∼11 according to Fig. 2f. The intensity peaked at pH = 4 and showed a slightly decrease at pH = 2. Meanwhile, the intensity maintained steady from 4–11 and experienced a dramatic drop to around 68 % at pH = 12, which means CDs emission unstable in strong alkaline. In addition, QY was calculated as section 2.6 depicted and the value turned out to be 4.5 %.

To determine the size of the carbon dots, TEM image and hydrated size were detected firstly. As Fig. 3 a shown, the carbon dots are well dispersed and have an average diameter of 1.91 nm under TEM image. The HRTEM image (Fig. 3b) showed the lattice distance was around 0.223 nm, which suggested a graphite plane [32]. The hydrated size result presents two main peaks located at 11.2 nm and 48.4 nm separately, which indicate a polydispersity of the colloid. This may because the dialysis bag can only filter the particle size under 220 nm. Considering the zeta potential of −15.0 mV, the particle couldn't reach the deflocculation state (usually absolute value reach 40–50 mV or more). As a result, there may be some agminated particles which were around 50 nm. Some researchers suggested that particles under 10 nm have certain possibility to going through the bacterial inner since bacterial cell walls are considerable compact [15,25]. Secondly, functional groups on the carbon dots were analyzed using FT-IR spectra. The difference of peaks between carbon dots and their precursors can be seen at Fig. 3c. Primary amino characteristic peaks (3357, 3296 cm−1) coming from Arg and dissociative hydroxyl (3493 cm−1) coming from CA were disappeared at the spectrum of carbon dots, while carboxyl characteristic peaks (3500∼2500 cm−1) maintained [33,34] for carbon dots (CA + Arg). Carbonyl stretching vibration had a red shift from around 1717 to 1657 cm−1, while some peaks were covered at 1650∼1300 cm−1 [35]. And C–O or C–N bending vibration (1183, 1130 cm−1; 1171, 1138 cm−1) weakened (may contained C–C or C–N bending vibration) after the reaction [27]. According to the red shift of CO and disappearance of primary amino and dissociative hydroxyl, peaks at 1563,1555 and 1507 cm −1 is considered as secondary amide peaks [27,35]. Peaks at 766 cm −1 (CA) and 769 cm −1 (Arg) refer to O–H swinging vibration and N–H swinging vibration respectively. These two peaks were weakened when forming carbon dots as Fig. 2b (CA + Arg) shown. It indicates the dissociated carboxyl group and amino group combined with other groups after. Besides, peaks at 750∼700 cm−1 usually refers to carbon skeleton vibration for chain structure [34]. The decreasing intensity of these peaks suggested the decomposition of chain structure of precursors which correspond to the formation of carbon dots.

To further confirm our results of FT-IR, XPS spectra were studied in Fig. 4 a ∼ 4d. The atom ratio was C 63.97 %, N 14.19 % and O 15.23 %, which shows carbon was the main atom. As Fig. 4b shown, there are C–C (284.93 eV), C–N/C–O (286.02 eV) and CO (288.08 eV) functional groups confirming the existence of carboxyl in carbon dots (C–O and CO) which is corresponding with the FT-IR spectra of 3500∼2500 cm−1 [33]. In Fig. 4c, N–C bond (399.82 eV) was the main bond compared with N–H bond (401.24 eV). However, the FT-IR result showed the disappearance of primary amino groups, that means the N–H attribute to secondary amine, not attribute to primary amine [20]. C–O bond (531.04 eV) and CO (532.19 eV) bond also be presented at Fig. 4d which proved there still some carboxyl retained after the reaction [30,33,36]. Both FT-IR spectra and XPS spectra indicate that there are carboxyl residues on the carbon dots and the disappearance of primary amino. The carboxyl residues can provide negative charge a [37–39]. And the transformation from primary amino to second amino means the formation of carbon dots go through the change of primary amino to form bond between citric acid and arginine. More importantly, the loss of primary amino weaken the antimicrobial performance of arginine and make the carbon dots less affinity to alive bacterial cells [40], providing potentials for live/dead cells discrimination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial imaging and alive/dead bacterial distinguishing
ce:section.ce:para: Since small size and carboxyl-contained nanoparticles show the potential this CDs to go through the bacteria cells [41,42], this kind of carbon dots were employed to imaging for dead bacterial cells firstly. Fig. 5 shows that the staining results of dead cells under fluorescent irradiation could totally match with the image under light. The merged images were put the light one, green one and red one together and used a color filter to delete the black background, which also proved the efficient ability of fluorescence staining dead E. coli cells for this carbon dots. The successful fluorescent staining can be due to the relevant small size of this carbon dots which the average size is 1.91 nm at TEM image and the hydrated size is 11.2 nm. This small-scale particles would go through the bacteria with a broken cell wall or membrane [40]. The same behaviors for bacterial staining for gram-positive bacteria are shown in Fig. 5, which means carbon dots all works for both gram-type bacteria.

Next, an application for alive/dead bacterial discrimination was put into practice. Dead cells and alive ones were mixed firstly. Then, the carbon dots colloid was added into the mixture for coculturing. After washing out the carbon dots colloid, the mixture was redissolved and filmed for observing. As Fig. 5 shown, the bacteria were shot under light just as normal. Fig. 5 recorded the fluorescent staining behaviors under fluorescence irradiation. Difference for staining were observed apparently between fluorescent images and corresponding merged ones, where some bacteria are stained and some are not. According to the result of dead cell staining, the unstained cells were highly possible alive ones which means the discrimination of alive/dead E. coli cells were performed successfully. Small size and functional groups such as carboxyl group and acylamino group on the carbon dots may be the two reasons for staining dead bacteria [26,37–39,41,42]. Because dead cells usually have incomplete bacterial cell walls and lose the surface charge to protect themselves, the carbon dots could stick to or go through them since their small size in a certain degree compared to the volume of bacteria [14,26]. The reason of the carbon dots only staining dead ones may be that the alive ones have integrated cell walls and functional negative charge on its surface, which inhibit our negative-charged carbon dots (−15.0 mV) to stick to them [26,27]. Moreover, cytotoxicity test was performed to prove that the carbon dots do not have significant toxicity to bacterial cells without light radiation (Fig. S2). And phototoxicity experiments were employed. As Fig. 6 a shown, cellular viability has not influenced much by white light radiation. However, ROS level was enhanced with the increase of carbon dots (CDs) concentration (Fig. 6b). And the survival of bacteria seemed not influenced by white light radiation. In a comprehensive perspective, though high concentration of carbon dots (over 250 μg/mL) induced generation of ROS, the phototoxicity of the carbon dots was very limited since bacterial viability and survival has not been influence very obviously (Fig. 6c). It indicates that the possibility of false positive results is very small. In other words, this kind of carbon dots will not inhibit the growth of bacteria and can hardly contribute to confusing result in dead/alive discrimination.

A kind of metal-free carbon dots were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid and arginine as precursors. Super small size provides potentials for the carbon dots to penetrate bacterial cells since particles under 10 nm could enter bacterial cells possibly [15,19,25]. And the luminescent properties make them suitable candidates for fluorescent imaging. In addition, carboxyl residues on the surface of the CDs provide negative charges, acting as a repellant that keeps them away from live bacterial cells with integrated cell walls. This selective repulsion ensures that the carbon dots do not stain the viable bacterial cells. Further, cytotoxicity results showed that carbon dots did not have significant bacterial killing ability whether with or without light irradiation, which proves there is scarcely possibility for staining bacterial cells after killing them. It is indicated that this kind of carbon dots can meet the need for alternatively staining dead bacterial cells even with the exist of the alive cells. Thereby, it can distinguish alive and dead bacterial cells clearly without false-positive results. Comparing with genotoxic small molecular dyes such as propidium iodide, this kind of carbon dots would be a potential dye for bacterial fluorescent imaging without obvious toxicity. The mechanism for the carbon dots on staining dead cells is passive selection rather than active seeking dead cells. Carbon dots with specific chemical groups that can recognize alive cells is urgent to be developed. Further investigation is warranted to elucidate the relationship between bacterial imaging and the specific chemical groups present in carbon dots. This research could pave the way for more targeted and effective applications in cell recognition and imaging.

Yuting Liu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Yuanhong Xu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision. Qin Wen: Investigation, Software, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274084), Natural Science Foundation of Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province (ZR2021JQ04), Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province (No. Tsqn 201909088).
